{{Short description|SI unit of energy}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Joule
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Energy]], [[Work (physics)]], [[International System of Units]]
| unit_system = [[SI derived unit]]
| unit_of = [[Energy]]
| symbol = J
| named_after = [[James Prescott Joule]]
| conversion = 1 J = 1 kg·m²/s² = 1 N·m = 1 W·s = 1 C·V
}}

The '''joule''' (symbol: '''J''') is the [[SI derived unit]] of [[energy]], [[Work (physics)|work]], and [[heat]]. It is named after the English physicist [[James Prescott Joule]] (1818–1889). One joule is defined as the amount of work done when a force of one [[newton (unit)|newton]] displaces a mass through a distance of one [[metre]] in the direction of the force:

:<math>1\ \text{J} = 1\ \text{N} \cdot \text{m} = 1\ \text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2 / \text{s}^2</math>

In electrical terms, one joule is the energy dissipated as heat when an [[electric current]] of one [[ampere]] passes through a [[electrical resistance|resistance]] of one [[ohm]] for one [[second]]. In [[thermodynamics]], one joule is equivalent to the work required to produce one [[watt]] of [[power (physics)|power]] for one second: 1 J = 1 W·s.

== Multiples and conversions ==
{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Submultiples !! Multiples
|-
| 1 microjoule (μJ) = 10⁻⁶ J || 1 kilojoule (kJ) = 10³ J
|-
| 1 millijoule (mJ) = 10⁻³ J || 1 megajoule (MJ) = 10⁶ J
|-
| || 1 gigajoule (GJ) = 10⁹ J
|}

=== Common conversions ===
* 1 [[calorie]] (thermochemical) ≈ 4.184 J
* 1 [[electronvolt]] (eV) ≈ 1.602×10⁻¹⁹ J
* 1 [[kilowatt-hour]] (kWh) = 3.6×10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ
* 1 [[British thermal unit]] (BTU) ≈ 1,055 J
* 1 [[erg]] = 10⁻⁷ J

== Relation to other physical quantities ==

=== Work ===
{{Main|Work (physics)}}
The work <math>W</math> done by a constant force <math>\mathbf{F}</math> over displacement <math>\Delta\mathbf{r}</math>:
:<math>W = \mathbf{F} \cdot \Delta\mathbf{r} = F\,\Delta r\cos\theta</math>

=== Kinetic energy ===
{{Main|Kinetic energy}}
:<math>E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2</math>
A 1 kg mass moving at √2 m/s has a kinetic energy of 1 J.

=== Heat ===
{{Main|Thermodynamics}}
The mechanical equivalent of heat, as measured by Joule: approximately 4.18 J raises 1 g of water by 1°C.

== Practical examples ==
* Energy required to lift a 100 g apple 1 m vertically: ~1 J
* Energy of a typical [[AA battery]]: ~10⁴ J
* Daily food energy intake (adult): ~8–12 MJ (8,000–12,000 kJ)
* Energy released in the [[Hiroshima bomb]]: ~6.3×10¹³ J

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Energy]]
* [[International System of Units]]
* [[Kinetic energy]]
* [[Newton (unit)]]
* [[Power (physics)]]
* [[Watt]]
* [[Work (physics)]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Units of energy]]
[[Category:SI derived units]]
[[Category:James Prescott Joule]]
